element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Player chooses between American (Washington, Ward, Lee) or British (Gage, Clinton, Howe) leadership with distinct strategic asymmetries
Continental Congress or Parliament meets quarterly to allocate funds across navy, districts, debts, salaries; political approval directly gates military spending
Purchase materials, bribe enemy generals, hold parades for morale, train troops; autonomous commanders can manage districts independently
Patriot/Loyalist militia and German mercenaries operate independently, collect resources, attack adjacent districts; can be recruited or absorbed by regular commanders
Combat occurs on terrain-varied hex grids with seasonal changes; unit placement determined by district entry vector; adjacent allies can reinforce
Infantry (basic), Guerrillas (terrain-mobile, surprise attacks), Cavalry (open terrain, shock charges), Engineers (cannons, bridge manipulation); naval barrage in coastal districts
Navy controlled strategically; provides coastal transportation for troops and naval artillery support; coastal access requirements affect deployment options
Civilian/political entity relationships determine budget allocation; in worst case, player can be voted out as Commander-in-Chief

typical_relief_triggers: Political approval gains from parades/public events
Quarterly budget allocation periods allowing resource reorganization
Recruitment of autonomous militia units to strengthen force
typical_tension_triggers: Quarterly political reviews (approval can lead to ouster)
Enemy commander activity in adjacent districts
Resource scarcity (debt accumulation, limited budget allocation)
Tactical battles where supply/attrition becomes critical
Loss of districts to enemy or militia defection
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Cyclical turn-based with quarterly political tension peaks; battles interrupt strategic resource-management phases; strategic side emphasizes long-term attrition over immediate combat resolution

spatial_grammar.optional_content_signal: Autonomous militia regiments naturally occupy unclaimed districts, signaling available territory for expansion or strategic positioning

difficulty_curve.early_game: Asymmetrical starting positions: Americans must survive and accumulate strength; British must fortify and move decisively. Budget constraints create early resource scarcity.
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Political approval becomes critical; autonomous militia forces interact unpredictably with player strategy; multi-district coordination required
difficulty_curve.late_game: Escalating attrition warfare; players may spend 250+ hours and still face defeat. Victory requires maintaining political support while managing nationwide military logistics.
notable_spikes: Quarterly political review votes (risk of ouster)
First major battle (combat system complexity spike)

notable_level_patterns.description: American side plays defensive-accumulation (hold territory, build strength gradually); British side plays aggressive-consolidation (fortify positions, rapid strikes). Same map/timeline, fundamentally different gameplay loops.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Full campaign playthrough (24 turns/year, multiple years)

notable_level_patterns.description: Districts function as persistent strategic resources generating income, supplies, and troop pools. Capture/retention drives economic and military power, not spatial progression.
Battles triggered by moving commanders into contested districts; army placement determined by entry vector and terrain; reinforcements from adjacent friendly districts create dynamic tactical depth.
